html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirty-Eight -- Complete

Pesher Nahum

4QpNAH -- 4Q169

A Qumran Commentary on the Prophet Nakhum -- DSS Attested

Fragments 1 through 4

Demetrius King of Greece -- The Furious Young Lion -- He Used to Hang Men Alive -- The Seekers-After-Smooth-Things -- Their Dominion Shall Cease -- The Simple of Ephraim Shall Return -- Creator Shall Judge All Nations

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 4 Qumran -- 4Q169 -- Hebrew Primary

The Only Qumran Pesher to Name Historical Figures

Demetrius III and Alexander Jannaeus -- Hasmonean Period

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

PESHER NAHUM -- 4QpNah -- FRAGMENTS 1 THROUGH 4

PESHER NAHUM — FRAGMENT 1 The Interpretation of Nakhum — Where the Lion Went to Bring Enough for His Cubs — Demetrius King of Greece — The Furious Young Lion Who Hangs Men Alive

1 Where the lion went to find food there the lion's cub and none shall disturb.

2 Its interpretation concerns Demetrius king of Greece who sought to enter Yerushalayim on the counsel of the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things.

3 But Creator did not give Yerushalayim into the hand of the kings of Greece from the time of Antiochus until the rise of the rulers of the Kittim.

4 But afterward it shall be trampled and given into the hand of the rulers of the Kittim.

5 The lion tears enough for his cubs and strangles prey for his lionesses.

6 Its interpretation concerns the Furious Young Lion who attacks with his great men and the men of his counsel.

7 And he fills his cave with prey and his den with torn flesh.

8 Its interpretation concerns the Furious Young Lion who executes revenge on the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things.

9 For he used to hang men alive — a thing never done in Yisra'EL before.

10 For concerning one hung alive on a tree it is written in the Torah: Behold I am against you says YHWH of hosts.

11 And I will burn your abundance in smoke and the sword shall devour your young lions.

12 Its interpretation: the sword of the Kittim shall devour the great men of Yisra'EL and the young men of his council.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QpNah (4Q169) was found in Cave 4 at Qumran and is designated 4Q169. It is one of the most historically significant of all the Qumran pesharim because it is the only Qumran commentary that names actual historical figures — Demetrius king of Greece and Antiochus. These are identified as Demetrius III Eucaerus (95-88 BCE) and Antiochus IV Epiphanes (175-164 BCE). The reference to the Furious Young Lion who hangs men alive — crucifixion, used against eight hundred Pharisees by Alexander Jannaeus around 88 BCE — gives the text a precise historical anchor and establishes that the community was active during the Hasmonean period.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition does not preserve the Pesher Nahum. The covenant prophetic tradition of Nakhum — the burden of Nineveh, the complete destruction of the oppressor city — is preserved in full in the Orit Ge'ez canon. The Qumran community applies Nakhum's prophecy against Nineveh to the political powers of their own day: the Seleucid Greek kings and the Kittim — Rome — who will ultimately trample Yerushalayim. The covenant prophetic literature is not addressed to the past but decoded as speaking about the community's present.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Nakhum 2:11-13 provides the base text for Fragment 1 — where is the den of the lions, the feeding ground of the young lions? The Pesher Nahum reads each word of Nakhum as an encoded reference to contemporary political events. The Seekers-After-Smooth-Things — a consistent Qumran epithet for their opponents, most likely the Pharisees — are identified as those who invited Demetrius to enter Yerushalayim, a reference to the historical invitation of Demetrius III by Jewish opponents of Alexander Jannaeus.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — he used to hang men alive, a thing never done in Yisra'EL before. The Pesher Nahum's reference to crucifixion — hanging men alive on a tree — as a unique atrocity is one of the most historically precise statements in the Qumran corpus. Alexander Jannaeus crucified eight hundred Pharisees in 88 BCE. The covenant community's condemnation of this act places them in opposition to the Hasmonean establishment and confirms the historical context of their separation. The citation of the Torah prohibition against hanging — Devarim 21:22-23 — places the act within the covenant legal framework of divine curse.

PESHER NAHUM — FRAGMENT 2 Woe to the City of Blood — The Seekers-After-Smooth-Things — Their Dominion Over Yisra'EL — Walking in Lies and Falsehood — The End of Their Rule Is Coming

1 Woe to the city of blood, all full of lies and rapine, prey does not depart.

2 Its interpretation concerns the city of Ephraim, the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things at the end of days, who walk in lies and falsehood.

3 The crack of the whip, and rumble of the wheel, galloping horse and jolting chariot.

4 Its interpretation concerns the rulers of the Kittim who in council deliberate and commit iniquity and deception.

5 Charging horsemen, flashing swords, and glittering spears, hosts of slain, and heaps of carcasses.

6 Its interpretation: this is the dominion of the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things — there shall not be absent from their midst the sword of the nations.

7 There is no end to the corpses, they stumble over their corpses — all because of the multitude of harlotries of the well-favored harlot.

8 Its interpretation concerns those who lead Ephraim astray — with their false teaching and their lying tongue and deceitful lips they lead many astray.

9 Kings, princes, priests, and people along with the proselyte who joins them — all of them fall by the sword through the pride of their lies.

10 Behold I am against you says YHWH of hosts and I will lift your skirts over your face.

11 Its interpretation concerns those who will be put to shame at the end of days when their wickedness is revealed to all Yisra'EL.

12 Many shall understand their error and despise them and they shall be found contemptible to all the people of the covenant.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q169 Fragment 2 preserves the pesher on Nakhum 3:1-7. The epithet Seekers-After-Smooth-Things — Hebrew dorshei halakot — is one of the most consistent designations for the community's opponents in the Qumran literature. The term is a wordplay on dorshei halakhot — those who seek legal rulings — implying that their opponents provide smooth comfortable interpretations of the Torah rather than its demanding truth. The city of Ephraim — used here for the opponents' location or community — distinguishes them from the community of Yehudah with which the Qumran group identifies.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition preserves Nakhum in full. The prophetic pattern that Nakhum applies to Nineveh — the city of blood, full of lies — is here applied by the pesher to the covenant community's opponents in Yerushalayim. The covenant prophetic pattern is consistent: the city that fills itself with blood and deceit will be destroyed by the same violence it has wielded. The Seekers-After-Smooth-Things who teach lies will be put to shame when their wickedness is revealed — the judgment of Nakhum on Nineveh becomes the judgment on the corrupt covenant leadership.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Nakhum 3:1-7 provides the base text for Fragment 2 — woe to the bloody city, it is all full of lies and robbery. The pesher method applies each verse of Nakhum to the Qumran community's specific historical situation. The rulers of the Kittim — Rome — and the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things are presented as parallel threats: the external political threat and the internal covenant corruption work together against the faithful covenant community.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — many shall understand their error and despise them and they shall be found contemptible to all the people of the covenant. The Pesher Nahum anticipates a future moment of revelation when the community's opponents will be publicly exposed and the covenant people will turn against them. This eschatological reversal — the present humiliation of the faithful community gives way to the public vindication — is the same pattern the Community Rule and the Damascus Document establish. The pesher genre applies the covenant prophetic pattern of reversal to the specific political and religious actors of the Hasmonean period.

PESHER NAHUM — FRAGMENT 3 The Interpretation of the End — Ephraim Shall No Longer Be Deceived — The Simple Ones of Ephraim Shall Leave — Menasseh in the Last Days — The Glory of Yehudah Shall Be Revealed

1 Its interpretation at the end of days concerns all the nations. What remains of the people shall be given to the sword.

2 And the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things — their dominion over Yisra'EL shall cease.

3 The simple ones of Ephraim shall abandon those who lead them astray and join themselves to Yisra'EL.

4 And the wicked of Ephraim and Menasseh who are those who forsook the covenant shall give way.

5 And those who betrayed the covenant shall be given into the hands of the enemies of the nations, for a great judgment against all the peoples of the earth.

6 And Menasseh in the last days — his kingdom shall be humbled in Yisra'EL.

7 And in the day of his fall many shall fall with him and his mighty men shall be destroyed.

8 The glory of Yehudah shall be revealed and the simple among them shall rejoice over Yisra'EL.

9 At that time the knowledge of Creator shall overflow the earth like waters covering the sea.

10 And all the wicked of his people who deny the covenant shall be cut off and their memorial shall be no more.

11 And the faithful shall stand firm and the wise shall understand all things.

12 For Creator has decreed an end for all wickedness and his judgment shall not turn back.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q169 Fragment 3 preserves the pesher's eschatological conclusion. The identification of Ephraim and Menasseh as code names for opposing factions — most likely the Pharisees and Sadducees respectively — is one of the most important insights of the Pesher Nahum for understanding Second Temple covenant politics. The promise that the simple ones of Ephraim shall abandon their misleaders and join themselves to Yisra'EL — the covenant community — reflects the Qumran conviction that their opponents' followers were deceived rather than wicked and could be recovered.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition preserves the covenant prophetic tradition of Yesha'yahu 11:9 — the earth shall be full of the knowledge of YHWH as the waters cover the sea — which appears in verse 9 of Fragment 3. The Words of the Luminaries, the Community Rule, and the Pesher Nahum all share this eschatological vision: the final age will be characterised by universal covenant knowledge. The Qumran community understood itself as already participating in the advance of this covenant knowledge through their study and transmission of the covenant texts.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Yesha'yahu 11:9 provides the covenant foundation for verse 9 of Fragment 3. The Pesher Nahum applies the same eschatological knowledge-overflow to the specific historical outcome of the conflict with the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things: when they are removed, the knowledge of Creator will fill the earth. The removal of false covenant teaching is the precondition for the universal spread of true covenant knowledge.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Pesher Nahum's use of code names — Ephraim, Menasseh, the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things, the Furious Young Lion, the Kittim — is the characteristic feature of the Qumran pesher genre. The community deliberately concealed the identities of the historical figures they were addressing using covenant prophetic epithets. This concealment served both a protective function — preventing the identification of the scroll's political statements — and a theological function: it embedded the community's history within the covenant prophetic narrative, making their present moment a fulfilment of scripture.

PESHER NAHUM — FRAGMENT 4 Nakhum 3 — The Harlotry of the Great City — She Is Taken Away in Captivity — All Who See Her Shall Flee — The Interpretation Concerns the Kittim and the End of the Age

1 She is carried away, she goes into captivity, her slave girls moaning like doves, beating their breasts.

2 Its interpretation: they are the simple of Ephraim — those of the congregation of Seekers-After-Smooth-Things who shall be taken captive at the end of days.

3 Nineveh is like a pool of water from the days of old, yet they are fleeing — halt, halt, but none turns back.

4 Its interpretation: at the time that the dominion of the Kittim falls, the children of wickedness shall flee.

5 Plunder the silver, plunder the gold — there is no end to the treasure, no end to the mass of all precious things.

6 Its interpretation: the great wealth of the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things shall be plundered and their riches given to the men of the covenant at the end of days.

7 She is empty and void and waste, hearts melting and knees trembling, anguish in all loins.

8 Its interpretation: this concerns all the kings of Greece who shall tremble on account of the judgment of the end.

9 Where is the den of the lions where they fed their young and none disturbed?

10 Its interpretation: this is the city where the wicked priests gathered — their congregation shall not endure and their end is the judgment of the sword.

11 Behold your people are women in your midst — the gates of your land are wide open to your enemies, the fire has devoured your bars.

12 Its interpretation: at the end of days Creator shall judge all the nations and the faithful of the covenant shall see his salvation and rejoice forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q169 Fragment 4 concludes the Pesher Nahum. The final pesher verse — Creator shall judge all the nations and the faithful of the covenant shall see his salvation — is the eschatological resolution of the entire commentary. The covenant community is not merely interpreting historical events: they are decoding the prophetic text to show that the present age of tribulation under the Kittim and the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things will culminate in a divine judgment that vindicates the faithful and destroys the wicked. The Pesher Nahum is a covenant document of eschatological encouragement as much as historical commentary.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition preserves Nakhum in full, including the imagery of the great city emptied and desolated. The Pesher Nahum's application of Nakhum to the covenant community's present adversaries reflects the covenant prophetic principle — established throughout the Orit Ge'ez prophetic canon — that every prophecy of judgment on historical nations carries within it a pattern applicable to all subsequent covenant situations. Nakhum did not merely speak about Nineveh; he spoke about every city that fills itself with blood and deceit in every age.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Nakhum 3:7-19 provides the base text for Fragment 4. The desolation of Nineveh — empty, void, and waste — is applied by the pesher to the coming desolation of the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things and the kings of Greece. The covenant pattern is consistent: the oppressor who appears invincible is suddenly destroyed, and those who suffered under the oppressor witness the divine reversal that the covenant prophets promised.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Pesher Nahum is the most historically grounded document in the Qumran sectarian literature precisely because it names historical figures. This makes it invaluable for establishing the community's historical context — the Hasmonean period, the reigns of Alexander Jannaeus and his contemporaries — but it also means that the document's political commentary was directly relevant to specific people and events. The code names are not merely literary — they are protective. The community's criticism of the Hasmonean establishment could have had serious consequences had the documents been identified and their targets recognised.

PESHER NAHUM -- COMPLETE

Volume Thirty-Eight -- The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

CREATOR SHALL JUDGE ALL THE NATIONS

AND THE FAITHFUL OF THE COVENANT SHALL SEE HIS SALVATION AND REJOICE FOREVER

Pesher Nahum Fragment 4 -- Dead Sea Scrolls 4Q169
